title: 5 Free Apps Every High Schooler Needs for Group Projects
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Group projects are a nightmare. Someone doesn't reply to texts. Someone does their part wrong. Someone's file is in a different format. Someone disappears entirely. You end up doing all the work. These five free apps will fix that.

1. Google Drive (The Foundation)

Stop emailing files back and forth. Create a shared folder in Google Drive. Everyone drops their work in the same place. You can see version history (who changed what and when), leave comments, and work simultaneously on the same document.

The best feature: suggestion mode. Instead of deleting someone's work, suggest edits. They can accept or reject. This avoids hurt feelings and preserves good ideas.

Google Docs for writing, Google Slides for presentations, Google Sheets for data. All free with a school email address.

2. Slack or Discord (Better Than Group Chats)

Text messages get buried. Group chats are chaos. Slack (or Discord) organizes conversations into channels. Create a channel for 'project planning,' another for 'research links,' another for 'feedback.' Nothing gets lost.

Both are free. Both work on phones and computers. Both let you share files, pin important messages, and search old conversations. Stop losing track of decisions in endless text chains.

3. Trello or Notion (Track Who Does What)

Who is doing which part? When is it due? What's already finished? Write it down in Trello or Notion. Create cards for each task. Assign them to people. Set due dates. Move cards from 'To Do' to 'Doing' to 'Done.'

This prevents the 'I thought YOU were doing that' conversation. Everything is visible. Everything is tracked. No excuses.

Trello is simpler (just lists and cards). Notion is more powerful (databases, wikis, calendars). Both have free plans.

4. Zoom or Google Meet (For Meetings That Aren't Chaos)

Don't try to coordinate over text. Have a 15-minute video call at the start of the project to assign roles and set deadlines. Have another call in the middle to check progress. Have a final call to review the finished project.

Video calls are faster than typing. You can share your screen, show your work, and ask clarifying questions. Fifteen minutes of talking saves two hours of confused texting.

Both are free with a Google account. Use them.

5. Grammarly (Save Your Group From Embarrassment)

Someone in your group writes like they're texting their best friend. Their section is full of lowercase i's, missing commas, and sentence fragments. Your grade depends on their writing. Grammarly fixes it.

It's a free browser extension that checks spelling and grammar. It also suggests clearer wording. Install it. Run it over the final document. Your teacher will thank you.

Bonus: Use the 'tone detector' to make sure your emails to the teacher don't sound rude or demanding.

The Golden Rule of Group Projects

Assign a 'project manager' at the beginning. This person creates the shared folder, sets up the communication channel, and tracks deadlines. Rotate who plays this role. Everyone takes a turn being responsible for the group's organization.

If you use these apps, group projects go from nightmares to easy As. Stop emailing files. Stop losing texts. Get organized.
